_meta.notes: post-type-videos. Gated looth-lite on the embed only; chapter anchors carry lg-ts-link so chapters stay visible but the pointer-events:none dash rule closes the YT bypass. Transcript is human-cleaned from the supplied auto-caption (ASR errors fixed: StewMac, Cricut, X-Acto, silk-screen, Tru-Oil, Crox, adheres, sealing). Links callouts enriched via WebSearch (jroadman.com, FB, StewMac ColorTone, Cricut, Silhouette, Tru-Oil, Crox Guitars).

blocks.tagline: Luthier James Roadman walks through making your own waterslide decals — paper types, artwork and resolution, printing and sealing, cutting with a Cricut, stencil tricks for metallics, and clean application.

blocks.html: Aired on Looth Group Live, Saturday, April 25th, 2026. With James Roadman of Roadman Guitars.

blocks.caption: Learn How to Make Decals, with James Roadman.

blocks.html:

blocks.label: Read the full transcript
blocks.body:
00:00:00 — Introduction & Ethics of Decal Replacement

Today we're talking about decals. There are any number of reasons you might want to print your own. You might be making a custom instrument with a custom logo and want the ability to make adjustments and produce your own. There are also reasons that come up with commercially made guitars: a replacement neck someone wants a logo on; an original neck with no logo; a guitar that comes in with the neck stripped, where your task is to refinish it and apply a new decal; or a repair where, to do the work, you have to strip the finish off and the decal gets damaged and needs replacing.

I bring this up at the start because there are times when people ask you to put a decal on something different from what the actual instrument or neck is. If you've been in the business a while you've probably encountered this and worked out your own red line on what's appropriate. For me it's hard and fast: I don't put a manufacturer's decal on an item that wasn't made by that manufacturer — and if it's a specific year, that goes too. You may not draw the same line, but it's worth thinking about ahead of time. Eventually someone will ask you to do something that makes you unsure, and if you've considered it in advance, that conversation is easier.

00:03:06 — What Are Water Slide Decals & Inkjet vs Laser Paper

A waterslide decal is a very thin transparent or white film mounted on paper with a water-soluble adhesive in between. The ones we're talking about are designed to go through a printer — either laser or inkjet. You make your design, print it onto the decal paper, and run it through. The processes differ slightly: not in the printing, but in the finishing, because inkjet ink is water-soluble and has to be coated to fix it before you apply the decal.

It's very important the decal paper matches the printer type — inkjet paper for an inkjet printer, laser paper for a laser printer. My experience is with inkjet, which has that extra sealing step; my understanding is the laser process is much the same minus the sealing. The nice thing about making your own is you can fit lots on a sheet, run a test before the real work, and you can afford to mess up if you have spares.

00:05:02 — Clear vs White Decal Paper & Color Limitations

Decal paper comes in white and clear. My experience is with clear, which is the majority of what guitar decals are made from. The ink is left behind and, on transparent paper, becomes virtually invisible. White paper works more like a regular sheet — vibrant, opaque colors — but you can't hide the edges the way you can with clear; you'd have to incorporate the edges into your artwork intentionally. I've never needed white paper for any of my projects.

Because clear is transparent, you'll see it slightly once applied, but once you spray finish over it, it disappears. That makes it work best on light-colored surfaces like maple. Where colors print opaque on white paper, on clear the ink is a little transparent, so the result combines the transparent color with the wood behind it — some colors look more vibrant for that reason. The unprinted areas are invisible, and there's a trick to take advantage of that later.

As for what colors are possible: black shows up as you'd expect; other colors show up transparent or semi-transparent. You have the full inkjet color set, gradients, even photographic images — though that's uncommon on the decals we're familiar with — and detail can get very fine, with small text reading clearly. What you can't do on clear is print white (inkjet can't print white); golds, silvers, and metallics — which manufacturers' decals use a lot — aren't possible, nor are neons and fluorescents.

00:08:45 — When to Make Your Own vs Buy from a Vendor

When does it make sense to make your own? If you need one or a couple, you're not planning to reuse it a lot, and it's relatively simple — or you just want to explore it. In restoration work where the original artwork isn't available: there are vendors who've done a lot of research on period-correct decals, but plenty of things aren't available and your only choice is to work out the artwork yourself. Sorting out the artwork is often the big part of the job; once that's done, it's not a huge step to make the decal too. Other reasons: precise color matching to your design, full creative control of size and layout, and being able to make as many as you want — it's not uncommon to tear one or place it badly, and if you haven't finished over it you can lift it off and try again.

When doesn't it make sense? When the era's artwork is genuinely hard to reproduce, it can be worth $20 and a button-press to have it arrive in the mail. Designs needing whites, metallics, or opaque colors call for a different manufacturing process. And time savings — if your time is worth more than sorting out all the details, just order it.

00:12:13 — Artwork: Vector vs Raster & Resolution Requirements

Artwork is the first hurdle. Whether you create your own or have someone make a logo, you can get two formats. Vector records shapes mathematically, so you can scale up or down with no loss of clarity — file formats include AI (Adobe Illustrator), SVG, and EPS (and possibly vector PDF). Raster, or pixel-based, looks clear until you zoom in and it goes grainy, because there aren't enough pixels to fill the space. If you have the option, vector is the way to go.

You can use raster as long as the pixel density is high enough. Printing is measured in DPI (dots per inch); screens in pixels per inch. Starting from a very high-density image and shrinking it down is no problem and gives greater detail — most decals are in the two-to-three-inch range. The problem is an image downloaded from the internet that was deliberately reduced for fast loading: print it and you get a blocky, pixelated result. To illustrate, I took my vector logo, rasterized it at the same size, and blew it up — from afar there's little difference, but up close the issues appear.

As a general rule, design at 300 DPI at the actual print size and you'll get a sharp image. There's some wiggle room, but 300 DPI is the number usually quoted in publishing — it's what a silk-screener friend requested for a headstock image I had screened for a Buck Owens. The nice thing about doing your own is you can print a test sheet on plain paper and know quickly whether the artwork is detailed enough. Most computer screens are only 72 to 96 DPI — worth remembering, because if you're hunting reference artwork off headstock photos to recreate a logo, I've never been able to download anything usable except as a guide to recreate the artwork on top of.

00:17:00 — Printing Settings & Sealing Inkjet Decals

Use decal paper made for your printer — in my case, inkjet. The paper is thick, akin to photo paper, so use photo-print settings: a higher-resolution print designed for glossy, thicker stock. Every printer is a little different. It's easy to print a test on plain paper first to verify size and placement, and you can get inkjet transparency paper to print a decal, set it in place, and preview the look. Once printed, let it rest so the ink dries securely, just like photo paper.

On laser versus inkjet: I don't have a laser printer, but my understanding is the toner is fused, so it doesn't need sealing after printing — though you still need the proper laser-specific waterslide paper. Inkjet ink is water-soluble, so it requires sealing; if you print, cut, and dunk an unsealed inkjet decal, the ink rolls right off and you'll know immediately. Sealing is easy and usually uses the same product you'll finish with. My experience is almost exclusively lacquer — I like the StewMac ColorTone clear rattle can. It's thin and easy to mist. I start with a very light mist coat, then slightly heavier coats — usually three very light coats to seal the ink in — then set it aside to cure until I'm ready to cut. Don't rush it; going too heavy causes problems.

00:20:09 — Cutting: Scissors, X-Acto & the Cricut Machine

Cutting is surprisingly not easy. We're all familiar with Fender-style logos that have a nice bubble outline — that's hard to do by hand unless you have mad scissor skills. The good news is that on most decals the edge doesn't matter much; it largely disappears under the finish, so an imperfect cut isn't a big deal. An X-Acto knife may be a better option, and use a straight edge for straight edges.

For something like a Fender decal applied over the finish, where you're going for a specific look in a restoration, you may want a mechanical cutter. Most people are familiar with the Cricut; other manufacturers make these machines too. I recently got one — they're useful and fun, and I'd recommend investing in one for this kind of work. With the Cricut you print from its software, and the machine uses reference marks on the outside to know where the artwork is; then you define where the cut goes. On some of the decals I made for this demo you can see a sweeping cut around the outside, and on the Looth Group one it's a perfect circle. The advantage is you can design exactly where the cutter goes, work out the cut once, and recreate it every time.

00:22:41 — Using Stencils to Add Metallics & Background Colors

I said earlier that a number of colors aren't possible with homemade waterslide decals, but getting the mechanical cutter got me thinking about workarounds. Because the decals are transparent, it opens the possibility of adding background colors, and the cutting machine lets you make very accurate stencils for them. I did one quickly — it's not sealed, but it shows the effect — and there's a lot of room for exploration: multiple colors or metallics could make some striking decals. There are limits; very complex logos would be hard to pull off, so you'd design around the technique.

Here's what I did: I printed the logo on transparency paper, then went into the Cricut software, plotted the areas I wanted in gold, and had it cut a stencil. The transparency let me locate where I wanted the mask before adhering it. Placement wasn't critical for the demo, but I was imagining a real use case. I cut out where I wanted color — you could do this with an X-Acto if the design is simple enough — and made sure it was completely nailed down before spraying; I put a little fish glue behind the center bit to hold it. After the lacquer dried it was easy to remove the stencil with a wet paper towel. Go with thin coats so you don't get paint beyond the stencil edges. The result worked surprisingly well and gave me lots of ideas for future uses.

00:26:15 — Applying the Decal: Surface Prep & Water Sliding

Apply the decal over a finished surface, not bare wood, and make it very smooth. I spray enough finish to level it flat, then one more flash coat for a clean, glossy surface. You don't want voids, scratches, or anything that would show as an air space under the decal once it's cured.

To release the decal from the backing, soak it in room-temperature or warm water — typically about 30 to 60 seconds. Don't overdo it. Be gentle: the film is very thin and easy to tear, especially a long decal with thin sections. Put a thin coat of water on the surface you're sliding onto, then gently slide the decal off the backing. The adhesive releases, and the water lets you slide it around to position it before it adheres. While the surface is wet you can reposition; then dab gently with a paper towel — some people use a small rubber squeegee — to work out air bubbles and remaining water. Use very, very light pressure. In the demo video the soak is shown in real time: 30 to 60 seconds, then the decal slides freely, and as the water is worked out it stops sliding and stays put where you want it.

00:31:45 — Warning: Not All Decals Are Equal

A warning: not all decals are equal. It's common for someone to bring in a project with a decal they sourced elsewhere. New-old-stock decals — printed long ago and floating around in someone's drawer — can be really fragile, and I've torn them. If you don't know the source of a decal, or you don't have a spare in case of a problem, be hyper-vigilant.

00:32:47 — Finishing Over the Decal

Finishing is just finishing over it with a little extra so you can level-sand. Decals cover remarkably easily; the edges disappear and become invisible. Use whatever your standard clear-coat schedule is, then focus a little extra finish on the decaled area so you can level-sand it flat.

00:33:38 — Q&A: Sealing with Matching Finish, Tru-Oil & Decal Paper Sources

Does it make sense to seal the decal with whatever finish the guitar was originally finished in — say poly on a poly-finish guitar — since finishes yellow differently? Yes — I'd use whatever finish you're using for the whole instrument. My experience is always lacquer, but poly would be the logical choice on a poly finish.

An attendee noted good luck using Tru-Oil over waterslide decals. Building the edge up to seal it in and make it invisible isn't difficult — a dozen coats or so. One used an inexpensive acrylic gloss spray from a hobby shop and then Tru-Oil over everything; it's a cheap, fast-drying, non-yellowing sealer used on many projects. Hobby shops are a good local source for both the decals and the papers; much can also be sourced online. For papers that print yellow, white, or gold/metallic, you'd buy those rather than make them — there are times it just doesn't make sense to make your own, but it's a good tool to have in the toolbox.

00:36:15 — Silk Screening onto Decal Paper & Cricut vs Silhouette

Another thing I've done: a friend with a screen-printing business. If you've sorted out the artwork and know someone who screen-prints, you can have them silk-screen onto the same decal paper. For a Buck Owens headstock restoration I had him silk-screen the actual headstock, and since the screen was made, I had him run a bunch of decals in case it came up later.

On machines: an attendee mentioned Silhouette as a competitor whose software some prefer. These machines are designed for crafting — cards and such — and the Cricut isn't very technical in the way I'd want; some terminology doesn't immediately make sense, and I had to do some plotting to work it out, though it does import vector files well. Another use mentioned was cutting clear pickguards for guitars. It's not a must-have — if you already have access to one, it falls into the “here, honey, this is a present for you” category.

00:39:09 — Cricut for Shielding & Headstock Repair Sourcing

You can also use the cutter for shielding — cut a custom cavity shield perfectly and drop it in, even for the back of a control cavity. If you don't have a big enough sheet, lay regular shielding tape side by side, cut it, and peel it off as one piece.

On headstock repairs where the original headstock and logo are damaged and you're completely refinishing: either make or source a new decal. Sometimes you source from the manufacturer (e.g. Fender) but usually it's aftermarket. One well-regarded source — suggested by David Strowy — is Crox Guitars (C-R-O-X) in England, which has a remarkably comprehensive decal library and a strong reputation; prices are reasonable, so if time matters that's often the easiest route. But if it's something you want to recreate yourself in black or printer-capable colors, the techniques here on an inkjet or laser printer will let you redo it.
